text: The city at night strips down to essentials. Streetlights cut sharp cones through dark air. Windows glow yellow, amber, the cold blue of screens. Traffic thins to occasional headlights sweeping corners. A delivery truck idles outside a restaurant. Somewhere a door opens and music spills out, then cuts off. The streets belong to different people now — the ones who work late, walk fast, move through shadows with purpose. Garbage bags line the curb waiting for morning. Distant sirens rise and fade. The city does not sleep so much as shift registers, swap one set of tasks for another, keep running through the dark with the lights left on.
